did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an enhanced Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) for skin cancer detection and classification. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images to classify seven skin lesion categories, achieving 94.0% validation accuracy. Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, were also employed to visualize the model's focus on pathological features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a custom CNN architecture integrated with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology including data augmentation on the HAM10000 dataset, model training, and evaluation metrics (94.0% validation accuracy, macro-averaged ROC-AUC of 0.996). It includes a Research Methodology section, results with confusion matrices and cross-validation, and a conclusion summarizing new contributions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhanced CNN with Special Attention Module and Explainable AI for Advanced Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Biomedical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology for Health (BECITHCON)
brief_summary: This study proposes an enhanced Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to address class imbalance and interpretability challenges in skin cancer detection. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, utilizing data augmentation and oversampling to handle severe class imbalance among seven skin lesion categories. Evaluation results demonstrated a validation accuracy of 94.0% and a macro-averaged ROC-AUC of 0.996, indicating high diagnostic power. Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, were employed to visualize the model's focus on clinically relevant pathological features, thereby enhancing transparency. The findings suggest that this lightweight, attention-based framework improves both accuracy and trustworthiness for computer-aided dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Majority class in the HAM10000 dataset with 6,705 samples; used in classification task.
Malignant class; specific focus on reducing false negatives; 1,186 samples in the test set.
Benign class; 1,181 samples in the test set; showed confusion with 'nv' and 'mel'.
Malignant class; 1,076 samples in the test set.
Pre-cancerous/malignant class; 1,040 samples in the test set.
Benign class; 1,004 samples in the test set.
Smallest class in the HAM10000 dataset with 115 samples originally; 1,002 samples in the test set after augmentation.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin lesion categories (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model uses a custom CNN with CBAM and softmax output to predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (94.0%), macro-averaged ROC-AUC (0.996), precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, obtained from Kaggle. It contains 10,015 images across seven diagnostic categories with severe class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the enhanced CNN model after applying data augmentation and oversampling to balance classes, resulting in a balanced training set of 6,705 samples per class.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor performance during training (10% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation and confusion matrix generation (20% of the dataset).
use.other: Used for 5-fold cross-validation to assess robustness.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the robustness and generalization capabilities of the model across various data subsets.
To maintain class distribution when splitting the dataset into training (70%), testing (20%), and validation (10%) sets.
To handle class imbalance during model training by adjusting the loss function based on class frequencies.
To evaluate the model's diagnostic power and ability to discriminate between classes.
To visualize and analyze the number of correctly and incorrectly classified samples for each class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-averaged ROC-AUC, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices, with 5-fold cross-validation used to assess robustness.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved a macro-averaged ROC-AUC of 0.996. 5-fold cross-validation yielded an average accuracy of 92.54% (±0.44%) with AUC consistently exceeding 0.994.

HAM10000_Test_Set_Per_Class.metric: Per-Class Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Support
HAM10000_Test_Set_Per_Class.notes: Confusion analysis noted higher confusion between 'bkl' and 'nv' and occasional nevus-like melanoma misses. Melanoma false negatives were reduced compared to basic CNNs.

Comparative_Results.notes: The proposed model achieved the highest accuracy (94.0%) on HAM10000 among the listed works while maintaining a lower parameter count (4.3M) compared to several competitors.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM)

architecture_details.backbone: Custom CNN with five convolutional blocks
architecture_details.attention_module: CBAM (Channel and Spatial attention) integrated after blocks 2–5
architecture_details.attention_parameters: Reduction ratio (r) = 8 for channel attention MLP bottleneck
architecture_details.pooling_layers: Max pooling after each block (except final block which uses Global Average Pooling)
architecture_details.output_head: Global Average Pooling (GAP) followed by two dense layers and Softmax

training_details.loss_function: Categorical Cross-Entropy with class weighting (compute_class_weight from scikit-learn)

classification_or_output_head.description: Two dense layers with dropout regularization followed by GAP and softmax for multi-class classification

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Custom CNN with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM)
Liu et al. [7] (Multi-scale + ECA + PSA on ResNet)
Younas et al. [8] (AIR-CNN / Attention-Based Inception-Residual CNN)
Pacheco and Krohling [9] (CNN Ensemble: ResNet-152, VGG-19, PNASNet)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel values to the [0,1] range
Data augmentation including flipping (horizontal and vertical), rotation (±20%), zooming (±20%), cropping, and brightness adjustment (±20%)
Oversampling of minority classes to balance the dataset
Stratification of data splits to maintain class distribution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to minority classes via ImageDataGenerator: rotation (±45°), width and height shifting (±20%), shearing (±20%), zooming (±20%), and horizontal and vertical flipping.
Brightness adjustment (±20%) applied to minority classes.
Oversampling of minority classes to balance the dataset. Synthetic samples were generated using aggressive transformations to match the majority class count (6,705 samples per class).
Targeted data augmentation applied specifically to minority classes to address severe class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset.

conclusions: The proposed lightweight CNN integrated with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) achieved a validation accuracy of 94.0% and a macro-averaged ROC-AUC of 0.996 on the HAM10000 dataset, demonstrating high precision and strong diagnostic power.
The model effectively addressed class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset through comprehensive data augmentation and oversampling, resulting in a balanced training set of 6,705 samples per class.

Integration of Grad-CAM for explainable AI allowed for qualitative validation of the model's decision-making process, confirming that the model focuses on clinically significant pathological features rather than spurious correlations.

The proposed architecture (4.3M parameters) outperformed several existing models in terms of accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset while maintaining lower computational costs and parameter counts compared to heavier architectures like ResNet and DenseNet variants.
5-fold cross-validation confirmed the model's robust generalization capabilities, yielding an average accuracy of 92.54% (±0.44%) with AUC consistently exceeding 0.994.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding remaining confusion between classes such as benign keratosis (bkl) and melanocytic nevi (nv), as well as occasional nevus-like melanoma misses.

Future work is identified to reduce missed melanomas by adjusting parameters for clinical sensitivity, defining alternative loss functions (e.g., focal loss), and conducting clinician-in-the-loop studies for real-world deployment.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits severe class imbalance (e.g., 6,705 samples for Melanocytic nevi vs. 115 for Dermatofibroma), limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Class imbalance was addressed via oversampling and aggressive data augmentation (rotation, zooming, flipping, brightness adjustment) to balance classes to 6,705 samples per class, which may introduce synthetic bias and limit real-world robustness.

The model achieved a macro-averaged ROC-AUC of 0.996 and 94.0% validation accuracy, but these metrics may be inflated due to the synthetic balancing of the training data and lack of external validation.

There is notable confusion between Benign Keratosis (bkl) and Melanocytic Nevi (nv), with bkl showing lower precision (0.86) and recall (0.89), indicating potential clinical misclassification risks for benign lesions.

Melanoma (mel) classification showed a recall of 0.83, meaning 17% of melanoma cases were missed; the authors acknowledge this limitation and suggest future work on focal loss or clinician-in-the-loop studies.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, relying instead on descriptive statistics and 5-fold cross-validation (average accuracy 92.54% ± 0.44%).

Explainability was provided via Grad-CAM, which qualitatively validated that the model focuses on pathological features, but this does not quantitatively prove clinical trustworthiness or rule out spurious correlations entirely.

The model was trained from scratch without transfer learning, which may limit feature extraction capabilities compared to models pre-trained on larger datasets like ImageNet, despite the lightweight architecture (4.3M parameters).

No external validation on independent datasets (e.g., ISIC 2019 for final evaluation, though comparative results are cited) was performed by the authors themselves, limiting the assessment of real-world deployment readiness.

The study is a conference paper presented in 2025, which may have limited peer review depth compared to journal publications, and the authors acknowledge the need for 'clinician-in-the-loop studies' for future deployment.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (also referred to as ISIC 2018 in metadata) for all phases of the experiment. The dataset, containing 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven skin lesion categories, was subjected to stratified splitting into training (70%), validation (10%), and testing (20%) sets. To address severe class imbalance, the training set was balanced via oversampling and aggressive data augmentation to 6,705 samples per class. The model was trained on the balanced training set, validated on the held-out validation set, and tested on the held-out test set. Additionally, 5-fold cross-validation was performed on the entire dataset to assess robustness. No external or unseen datasets were used for testing by the authors; comparative results for other datasets (e.g., ISIC 2019) were cited from other studies.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset. While the paper cites results from other works on datasets like ISIC 2019, it does not report any experiments where its own model was trained on one dataset and tested on a different, unseen dataset.

training.notes: Used for model fitting. The original training split was balanced via oversampling and data augmentation to 6,705 samples per class for all 7 classes.

validation.notes: Used for model selection, monitoring performance during training, and early stopping.

testing.notes: Used for final internal evaluation, generating confusion matrices, and calculating per-class metrics.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Synthetic samples were generated for minority classes using ImageDataGenerator with aggressive transformations (rotation ±45°, shifting ±20%, shearing ±20%, zooming ±20%, flipping, brightness adjustment ±20%) to balance the training set to 6,705 samples per class.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: Stratified split: 70% training, 10% validation, 20% testing. Total dataset size: 10,015 images.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 5-fold cross-validation was performed on the entire dataset to assess robustness, yielding an average accuracy of 92.54% (±0.44%).
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used for testing. The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability due to the lack of external validation.

dataset_level_results.notes: Validation accuracy of 94.0% and macro-averaged ROC-AUC of 0.996 reported.
Per-class metrics on the 20% test set. Melanoma (mel) recall was 0.83.
5-fold cross-validation results: Average accuracy 92.54% (±0.44%), AUC consistently > 0.994.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which has severe class imbalance and limited diversity, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No external validation on independent datasets was performed by the authors, limiting the assessment of real-world deployment readiness.
The use of synthetic balancing via aggressive augmentation may introduce bias and limit real-world robustness.
The paper acknowledges the need for clinician-in-the-loop studies and external validation for future work.
information_not_reported: Exact patient-level separation (whether images from the same patient appear in both train and test sets) is not explicitly reported, though image-level splitting is implied.
Confidence intervals for the primary accuracy and AUC metrics are not reported, only standard deviation for CV accuracy.
Formal statistical hypothesis testing p-values are not reported.
